channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Baryonic Matter
item.description:
Baryonic matter is ordinary matter made of protons, neutrons, and electrons – the stuff of atoms, molecules, rocks, water, air, stars, planets, and living things. “Baryonic” refers to baryons (protons and neutrons), which carry nearly all the mass. Electrons are technically leptons, not baryons, but they are included in the category because they are bound to baryonic atoms and contribute negligibly to the total mass.

Baryonic matter is everything visible in the universe. Every photon ever collected by a telescope came from baryonic matter or its interactions. Despite this, baryonic matter constitutes only about 5% of the total energy content of the universe. The remaining 95% is dark matter (~27%) and dark energy (~68%).

item.title: Cosmic Microwave Background
item.description:
The cosmic microwave background (CMB) is thermal radiation left over from recombination, approximately 380,000 years after the Big Bang – the oldest light in the universe, now cooled by the expansion of space from roughly 3,000 kelvin to 2.725 kelvin, placing it in the microwave band of the electromagnetic spectrum.

Before recombination, the universe was a hot plasma of free electrons and atomic nuclei. Photons scattered constantly off free electrons and could not travel far – the universe was opaque, like the interior of a star. When the universe cooled enough for electrons to combine with nuclei into neutral atoms, photons were freed. The CMB is the light from that moment, arriving from every direction in the sky.

item.title: Cosmic Web
item.description:
The cosmic web is the large-scale pattern of matter distribution in the universe: a network of filaments connecting dense nodes (where galaxy clusters sit) and surrounding vast, nearly empty voids.

The web formed through gravitational amplification of tiny density fluctuations present in the early universe. Regions slightly denser than average attracted more matter, grew denser still, and eventually collapsed into structures. The dark matter collapsed first (because it does not interact electromagnetically and therefore does not resist compression through radiation pressure), forming a scaffolding along which baryonic matter subsequently collected. The result is a three-dimensional structure that looks, roughly, like a sponge.

item.title: Dark Energy
item.description:
Dark energy is the unknown component comprising approximately 68% of the universe’s total energy content, responsible for the observed accelerating expansion of space.

The simplest model of dark energy is the cosmological constant Λ\Lambda, a term in the Einstein field equations representing a constant energy density pervading all of space. Unlike dark matter, which clumps gravitationally and slows expansion, dark energy has a uniform density that does not dilute as space expands and exerts a negative pressure that drives accelerating expansion.

item.title: Dark Matter
item.description:
Dark matter is matter that interacts gravitationally but not electromagnetically – invisible to all telescopes across the entire electromagnetic spectrum, detectable only through its gravitational effects on visible matter and light.

The evidence for dark matter comes from multiple independent observations. Galaxy rotation curves: stars in the outer regions of spiral galaxies orbit faster than the visible mass can explain (Rubin and Ford, 1970s). Galaxy clusters: the galaxies move too fast to be held together by visible mass alone (Zwicky, 1933). Gravitational lensing: light from distant galaxies bends more than the visible mass of foreground clusters can account for. The cosmic microwave background: the pattern of temperature fluctuations requires a component that gravitates but does not interact with photons. Large-scale structure formation: without dark matter, the observed galaxies and clusters could not have formed in the time since the Big Bang.

item.title: Galaxy
item.description:
A galaxy is a gravitationally bound system of stars, gas, dust, and dark matter, ranging from dwarf galaxies containing as few as 10710^7 stars to giant ellipticals containing 101310^{13}.

Galaxies come in several morphological types. Spiral galaxies (like the Milky Way) have a flat rotating disk with spiral arms, a central bulge, and a surrounding halo of dark matter and old stars. Elliptical galaxies are roughly spheroidal collections of old stars with little gas or dust. Irregular galaxies lack coherent structure, often because of gravitational interactions with neighbors.

item.title: Galaxy Cluster
item.description:
A galaxy cluster is the largest gravitationally bound structure in the universe, containing hundreds to thousands of galaxies embedded in hot intracluster gas and dark matter halos.

The visible galaxies account for only a few percent of a cluster’s total mass. The hot gas between galaxies (the intracluster medium), detectable in X-rays, contributes roughly 15%. The remaining 80% or more is dark matter. Galaxy clusters are among the strongest pieces of evidence for dark matter: the gravitational mass needed to hold the cluster together far exceeds the mass visible in stars and gas. Fritz Zwicky first noticed this discrepancy in the Coma Cluster in 1933, calling the missing component “dunkle Materie.”

item.title: Star
item.description:
A star is a gravitationally bound sphere of plasma that generates energy through nuclear fusion in its core, converting lighter elements into heavier ones and radiating the resulting energy as light.

Stars form when clouds of gas (primarily hydrogen) collapse under their own gravity. As the cloud contracts, its core heats up. When the core reaches approximately 15 million kelvin, hydrogen nuclei begin fusing into helium, releasing energy that creates an outward pressure balancing gravitational collapse. This equilibrium – hydrostatic equilibrium – defines a star’s main-sequence lifetime.
